title: Harvesting fibre from Angora and cashmere goats
subtitle: Shearing Angoras and combing cashmere from mixed-fibre goats.
excerpt: Angora goats produce mohair twice a year and must be shorn on a schedule to prevent the fleece becoming matted. Cashmere goats, including many Pygora and crossbred fibre goats, shed their fine undercoat in spring and can be combed or plucked rather than shorn. The methods and timing are quite different for each.
sourceNotes: British Angora Goat Society management guidance, bagsonline.co.uk. Cashmere and Camelid Fibre Producers, UK. SAC Consulting small ruminant fibre production notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fine lustrous fibre produced by Angora goats. High in tensile strength and natural sheen. Harvested by shearing twice yearly.
The soft fine downy undercoat that many goats, including Cashmere and Pygora breeds, produce in autumn and shed in spring. Separated from the coarser outer coat by combing.
The length of a single lock of wool or mohair from the base to the tip. Angora mohair for handspinning is best at 10 to 15 cm, harvested at 6 to 8 months' growth.

content.text: The two fibre goat types require different harvest methods. Angora goats produce
that must be shorn, it does not self-shed. Cashmere and cashmere-cross breeds produce a fine
beneath a coarser outer coat; the undercoat loosens in spring and can be combed or plucked free as it releases.
Angoras are shorn twice a year: late February to March and again in August or September. The February shearing is the most critical, the
at six months' growth is typically 10 to 15 cm, ideal for handspinning and for commercial processing.

Do not shear in wet weather. Angoras chill quickly after shearing; a freshly shorn Angora in cold wet conditions can die from hypothermia within hours. Shear in a covered space and keep the goats housed overnight after shearing if temperatures are below 10°C.
The shearing technique is the same as for a sheep, see

, but with tighter blade control. Mohair with second cuts (short fibre fragments from a second pass of the shears) contaminates a handspinner's braid and reduces the value of commercial fleece. Take each blow cleanly with the shears and avoid going over an area twice.

Roll the fleece immediately after shearing with the cut side inward and fasten with paper twine, not baler twine (the polypropylene fibres contaminate the mohair). Store in a cool dry place in breathable sacks; plastic bags sweat and damage the fibre.

The cashmere undercoat releases in late February through April as daylight hours increase. When you see fibres drifting off the coat on a still day, or when the undercoat pulls free with light finger pressure, combing can begin.

Use a hand comb or dog slicker brush and work in sections from neck to rump. The undercoat comes away in clumps; the coarser guard hair stays attached. Work slowly in the direction of growth, moving down through the fleece rather than against it. A single session per side takes about twenty minutes per goat.

Cashmere yield from a single smallholding goat is typically 100 to 300 g of usable fibre per year after removing guard hair. This is a small quantity but is valuable. A Pygora or Cashgora cross will yield more than a generic fibre goat but less than a true Cashmere. Know your breed's realistic output before investing in processing equipment.
